It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 125 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 4.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
After moving DOWN 3 and RIGHT 3:
- White: rows 38-52, cols 44-58 (135px) - moved to the bottom-right area
- Green (N): rows 32-40, cols 38-58 (117px) and rows 38-40, cols 50-52 (9px)
- Sky blue: rows 35-37, cols 41-46, 47-49, 50-52, 53-55; rows 41-49, cols 47-55
- Red: rows 29-31, cols 44-46 and 50-52; rows 35-37, cols 47-49 and 53-55
Looking at the image, I see:
- A green frame at the top
- Red blocks inside the frame
- A white "P" shape at the bottom
- Sky blue blocks scattered
The white character is now at rows 38-52, cols 44-58. The initial green frame was at rows 29-49, cols 41-49. The character overlaps with the green frame area.
The white bar at the top is now 21px (was 18px before). So it increased by 3px for 6 actions, which is 0.5px per action. This is still very slow progress.
Let me look at the image more carefully. The white character is now in the bottom-right area, and there's a green frame above it with red blocks inside. The sky blue blocks are scattered.
Maybe the goal is to have the character overlap with the green frame in a specific way. Let me try moving the character UP to overlap more with the green frame.
Actually, let me look at the initial state again:
- White: rows 11-25, cols 11-25 (15x15)
- Green: rows 29-49, cols 41-49 (21x9)
- Sky blue: rows 32-46, cols 44-46 (15x3)
The green frame is 21 rows x 9 cols, and the sky blue is 15 rows x 3 cols inside it.
The white character is 15x15.
Maybe the goal is to move the character so that it overlaps with the sky blue region. The sky blue is at rows 32-46, cols 44-46.
Currently, the white character is at rows 38-52, cols 44-58. It overlaps with the sky blue region at rows 38-46, cols 44-46.
Let me try moving the character UP to align better with the sky blue region.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me look at the initial state and see if there's a pattern.
Initial state:
- White at (11-25, 11-25)
- Green at (29-49, 41-49)
- Sky blue at (32-46, 44-46)
- Red at (26-28, 14-16), (26-28, 20-22), (35-37, 38-40), (41-43, 38-40)
The white character is at the top-left, and the green frame is at the bottom-right. The sky blue is inside the green frame.
Maybe the goal is to move the character to the green frame's position and then click SPACE to "place" it there.
Let me try clicking SPACE now that the character is near the green frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White at rows 38-52, cols 44-58. Green frame above at rows 32-40.
# Let me try clicking SPACE to see if placing the character completes the level

action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After SPACE:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 5:
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Node ({node['color']}): rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, {node['pixels']}px")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
